title_en: Chapter on Calling to the Testimony of Faith
title_ar: باب الدعوة إلى شهادة أن لا إله إلا الله
content_en:
This chapter examines the obligation and methodology of calling people to the testimony of faith — La ilaha illallah, Muhammadun Rasulullah — as the first and most fundamental element of Islamic invitation (da'wah). Ibn Abd al-Wahhab grounds this discussion in the famous hadith of Mu'adh ibn Jabal, recorded in both al-Bukhari and Muslim, in which the Prophet ﷺ sent him to Yemen with specific instructions about the priority and sequence of da'wah.

The Prophet ﷺ told Mu'adh: 'You are going to a people of the Book. Let the first thing you call them to be the testimony that there is no deity worthy of worship except Allah.' In another narration, the wording is: 'the worship of Allah alone (Tawhid).' The scholars of Islam have used this hadith to establish the principle of da'wah in stages — beginning with the correction of one's fundamental relationship with Allah before moving on to the secondary obligations of the Sharia. Prayer, zakat, fasting, and hajj are all meritorious and obligatory, but they are rendered hollow without the proper foundation of Tawhid. One cannot bring a gift into a king's court before being granted entry, and entry into worship that Allah accepts requires first establishing the correct understanding of who is being worshipped.

The chapter also engages with the verse: 'Say: This is my way; I call to Allah with insight — I and those who follow me' (Quran 12:108). The phrase 'with insight' (ala basirah) is critical. Da'wah to Allah must be grounded in knowledge — understanding what Tawhid means, understanding the various forms of shirk that contradict it, and understanding the condition of those being called. Blind or uninformed da'wah can do more harm than good, which is why the scholars have consistently emphasized that the caller to Islam must themselves be firmly grounded in the knowledge of what they are calling to.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab draws an important lesson from the Prophet's ﷺ instruction to Mu'adh: that even among Muslims, the call to Tawhid never becomes redundant. In Mu'adh's case, he was being sent to a Muslim region (Yemen had already accepted Islam), yet the Prophet ﷺ still instructed him to begin with Tawhid. This is because Tawhid is not merely an introductory lesson to be left behind once one enters Islam — it is the living root of every act of worship, and it requires constant renewal, deepening, and protection.

The chapter further elaborates that calling to the Shahada is not limited to verbal proclamation. It includes teaching its meaning, correcting misunderstandings about it, removing practices that contradict it, and modeling it in one's own life. The Prophet ﷺ spent thirteen years in Mecca calling primarily to Tawhid before the detailed laws of Islam were legislated. This Meccan phase of da'wah was not an inferior or incomplete form of Islam — it was laying the most essential foundation upon which everything else would stand.

For Ibn Abd al-Wahhab, this chapter carries particular urgency given the context in which he wrote. He observed widespread practices in the Arabian Peninsula of his era that he viewed as compromising the integrity of Tawhid — veneration of shrines, seeking intercession from the dead, and the invocation of other than Allah in times of distress. His call to return to pure Tawhid was, in his framing, precisely the same call that every prophet had made to their people.
